nodes.name: notebook.reset

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

Query: Could you create a 5-day trip itinerary for two, departing from Baltimore and visiting two cities in New York from March 6th to March 10th, 2022? Our budget is set at $2,200. We require accommodations that allow parties and should ideally be entire rooms. We'd like to avoid flight transportation.
Thought 1:

nodes.output: None

nodes.name: llm-thought-step6

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

nodes.name: llm-thought-step9

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

nodes.name: llm-thought-step11

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

Query: Could you create a 5-day trip itinerary for two, departing from Baltimore and visiting two cities in New York from March 6th to March 10th, 2022? Our budget is set at $2,200. We require accommodations that allow parties and should ideally be entire rooms. We'd like to avoid flight transportation.
Thought 1: Thought 1: The user wants a 5-day trip from Baltimore to two cities in New York State (not necessarily New York City) between March 6–10, 2022, without using flights. First, I need to identify suitable cities in New York State to visit. I’ll use CitySearch to find cities in New York.
Action 1: CitySearch[New York]
Observation 1: ['Buffalo', 'Manhattan', 'Niagara Falls', 'Islip', 'New York', 'Watertown', 'Newburgh', 'Syracuse', 'Plattsburgh', 'Ogdensburg', 'Ithaca', 'Elmira', 'White Plains', 'Albany', 'Binghamton', 'Rochester']
Thought 2: Thought 2: The user wants to visit two cities in New York State. From the list, "New York" likely refers to New York City (which includes Manhattan), and other notable options include Albany, Syracuse, Rochester, Buffalo, and Niagara Falls. Since the trip is only 5 days and ground transportation is preferred, I should prioritize cities that are reasonably close to Baltimore and to each other. I’ll first check driving details from Baltimore to New York City and then to a second nearby city like Albany or Philadelphia—but Philadelphia is not in New York State, so I’ll consider Albany as a second destination. I’ll start by checking the driving distance, time, and cost from Baltimore to New York City using self-driving mode.
Action 2: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Baltimore, New York, self-driving]
Observation 2: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 3: Thought 3: The drive from Baltimore to New York City takes about 3 hours, which is reasonable. Now I need to select a second city in New York State that’s accessible from New York City within the trip window. Albany is a logical choice—it’s the state capital and within driving distance. I’ll check the driving details from New York City to Albany.
Action 3: GoogleDistanceMatrix[New York, Albany, self-driving]
Observation 3: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 4: Thought 4: The GoogleDistanceMatrix query for New York to Albany may have failed due to ambiguity—“New York” could be interpreted as the state rather than the city. To ensure clarity, I should use “New York City” or “Manhattan” as the origin. Since “Manhattan” appeared in the CitySearch results, I’ll use that as a precise destination label. I’ll retry the distance query from Manhattan to Albany using self-driving mode.
Action 4: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Manhattan, Albany, self-driving]
Observation 4: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 5: Thought 5: It seems that using "Manhattan" may not be recognized as a valid origin in the GoogleDistanceMatrix tool. I should instead use "New York City" explicitly or stick with "New York" but ensure consistency. Since the initial CitySearch returned "New York" as a city, I’ll assume it refers to New York City and proceed by searching for accommodations, restaurants, and attractions in both New York City and Albany to build a robust itinerary. First, I’ll search for accommodations in New York City that allow parties and offer entire rooms.
Action 5: AccommodationSearch[New York]
Observation 5: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 6: Thought 6: The accommodation results for New York show several "Entire home/apt" options, but many explicitly prohibit parties. However, a few listings like "Cool Downtown Apartment in Great Location" and "Magical rowhouse and garden in Williamsburg" do not mention a "No parties" rule—though the latter says "No parties," so it's excluded. The "Cool Downtown Apartment" only restricts pets, which may be acceptable. I’ll note this and now search for accommodations in Albany with similar criteria.
Action 6: AccommodationSearch[Albany]
Observation 6: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 7: Thought 7: The accommodation results for Albany appear to be mislabeled—many listings reference Manhattan, Times Square, or Brooklyn, which are in New York City, not Albany. This suggests a data error or misclassification in the search results. To ensure accuracy, I should verify attractions and restaurants in both cities to confirm their viability as destinations, and possibly consider an alternative second city like Syracuse or Rochester if Albany data remains unreliable. However, given time constraints and proximity, I’ll proceed with New York City and assume the second destination could be Niagara Falls (a major attraction in NY State), but first I’ll gather dining and attraction options for New York City.
Action 7: RestaurantSearch[New York]
Observation 7: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 8: Thought 8: The restaurant results for "New York" appear inconsistent—they include names and cuisines that seem more aligned with Indian or generic global chains rather than authentic New York City dining options. This suggests possible data contamination or mislabeling in the RestaurantSearch tool. Despite this, I’ll proceed to gather attraction information for New York to confirm viable activities. After that, I’ll attempt to validate a second city—perhaps Niagara Falls, which is a well-known destination in New York State—and collect accommodation, dining, and attraction data there to ensure a feasible two-city itinerary within the budget and timeframe.
Action 8: AttractionSearch[New York]
Observation 8: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 9: Thought 9: The attraction results for "New York" clearly correspond to New York City, featuring iconic landmarks like the Empire State Building, Statue of Liberty, and Central Park. This confirms that "New York" in our context refers to New York City. Now I need a valid second city in New York State that is distinct and offers attractions, dining, and suitable accommodations. Niagara Falls is a strong candidate—it’s a major tourist destination within New York State and fits the trip duration if we plan driving legs efficiently. I’ll now search for accommodations in Niagara Falls that allow parties and offer entire rooms.
Action 9: AccommodationSearch[Niagara Falls]
Observation 9: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 10: Thought 10: The accommodation results for Niagara Falls appear mislabeled—many listings reference Manhattan, Brooklyn, or Bushwick, which are in New York City, not Niagara Falls. This indicates a systemic issue with location tagging in the AccommodationSearch tool. Despite this, one listing—"Private Guestroom in Landmark Bklyn Brownstone"—is labeled as "Entire home/apt" and located in "Niagara Falls" but clearly references Brooklyn. Given these inconsistencies, I should instead rely on cities that are unambiguously distinct and likely to yield accurate data. Syracuse or Rochester may be better alternatives, but given proximity and travel time from Baltimore, it may be more practical to focus the entire trip within New York City (which includes multiple boroughs and can be treated as offering diverse experiences) and one nearby upstate city with reliable data. However, since the user explicitly requested two cities in New York State, and New York City is confirmed via AttractionSearch, I’ll proceed by assuming the second city is Albany and collect attraction and restaurant data for it to validate its suitability—even if accommodation data was flawed.

nodes.name: llm-thought-step12

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

nodes.name: llm-thought-step14

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

Query: Could you create a 5-day trip itinerary for two, departing from Baltimore and visiting two cities in New York from March 6th to March 10th, 2022? Our budget is set at $2,200. We require accommodations that allow parties and should ideally be entire rooms. We'd like to avoid flight transportation.
Thought 1: Thought 1: The user wants a 5-day trip from Baltimore to two cities in New York State (not necessarily New York City) between March 6–10, 2022, without using flights. First, I need to identify suitable cities in New York State to visit. I’ll use CitySearch to find cities in New York.
Action 1: CitySearch[New York]
Observation 1: ['Buffalo', 'Manhattan', 'Niagara Falls', 'Islip', 'New York', 'Watertown', 'Newburgh', 'Syracuse', 'Plattsburgh', 'Ogdensburg', 'Ithaca', 'Elmira', 'White Plains', 'Albany', 'Binghamton', 'Rochester']
Thought 2: Thought 2: The user wants to visit two cities in New York State. From the list, "New York" likely refers to New York City (which includes Manhattan), and other notable options include Albany, Syracuse, Rochester, Buffalo, and Niagara Falls. Since the trip is only 5 days and ground transportation is preferred, I should prioritize cities that are reasonably close to Baltimore and to each other. I’ll first check driving details from Baltimore to New York City and then to a second nearby city like Albany or Philadelphia—but Philadelphia is not in New York State, so I’ll consider Albany as a second destination. I’ll start by checking the driving distance, time, and cost from Baltimore to New York City using self-driving mode.
Action 2: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Baltimore, New York, self-driving]
Observation 2: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 3: Thought 3: The drive from Baltimore to New York City takes about 3 hours, which is reasonable. Now I need to select a second city in New York State that’s accessible from New York City within the trip window. Albany is a logical choice—it’s the state capital and within driving distance. I’ll check the driving details from New York City to Albany.
Action 3: GoogleDistanceMatrix[New York, Albany, self-driving]
Observation 3: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 4: Thought 4: The GoogleDistanceMatrix query for New York to Albany may have failed due to ambiguity—“New York” could be interpreted as the state rather than the city. To ensure clarity, I should use “New York City” or “Manhattan” as the origin. Since “Manhattan” appeared in the CitySearch results, I’ll use that as a precise destination label. I’ll retry the distance query from Manhattan to Albany using self-driving mode.
Action 4: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Manhattan, Albany, self-driving]
Observation 4: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 5: Thought 5: It seems that using "Manhattan" may not be recognized as a valid origin in the GoogleDistanceMatrix tool. I should instead use "New York City" explicitly or stick with "New York" but ensure consistency. Since the initial CitySearch returned "New York" as a city, I’ll assume it refers to New York City and proceed by searching for accommodations, restaurants, and attractions in both New York City and Albany to build a robust itinerary. First, I’ll search for accommodations in New York City that allow parties and offer entire rooms.
Action 5: AccommodationSearch[New York]
Observation 5: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 6: Thought 6: The accommodation results for New York show several "Entire home/apt" options, but many explicitly prohibit parties. However, a few listings like "Cool Downtown Apartment in Great Location" and "Magical rowhouse and garden in Williamsburg" do not mention a "No parties" rule—though the latter says "No parties," so it's excluded. The "Cool Downtown Apartment" only restricts pets, which may be acceptable. I’ll note this and now search for accommodations in Albany with similar criteria.
Action 6: AccommodationSearch[Albany]
Observation 6: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 7: Thought 7: The accommodation results for Albany appear to be mislabeled—many listings reference Manhattan, Times Square, or Brooklyn, which are in New York City, not Albany. This suggests a data error or misclassification in the search results. To ensure accuracy, I should verify attractions and restaurants in both cities to confirm their viability as destinations, and possibly consider an alternative second city like Syracuse or Rochester if Albany data remains unreliable. However, given time constraints and proximity, I’ll proceed with New York City and assume the second destination could be Niagara Falls (a major attraction in NY State), but first I’ll gather dining and attraction options for New York City.
Action 7: RestaurantSearch[New York]
Observation 7: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 8: Thought 8: The restaurant results for "New York" appear inconsistent—they include names and cuisines that seem more aligned with Indian or generic global chains rather than authentic New York City dining options. This suggests possible data contamination or mislabeling in the RestaurantSearch tool. Despite this, I’ll proceed to gather attraction information for New York to confirm viable activities. After that, I’ll attempt to validate a second city—perhaps Niagara Falls, which is a well-known destination in New York State—and collect accommodation, dining, and attraction data there to ensure a feasible two-city itinerary within the budget and timeframe.
Action 8: AttractionSearch[New York]
Observation 8: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 9: Thought 9: The attraction results for "New York" clearly correspond to New York City, featuring iconic landmarks like the Empire State Building, Statue of Liberty, and Central Park. This confirms that "New York" in our context refers to New York City. Now I need a valid second city in New York State that is distinct and offers attractions, dining, and suitable accommodations. Niagara Falls is a strong candidate—it’s a major tourist destination within New York State and fits the trip duration if we plan driving legs efficiently. I’ll now search for accommodations in Niagara Falls that allow parties and offer entire rooms.
Action 9: AccommodationSearch[Niagara Falls]
Observation 9: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 10: Thought 10: The accommodation results for Niagara Falls appear mislabeled—many listings reference Manhattan, Brooklyn, or Bushwick, which are in New York City, not Niagara Falls. This indicates a systemic issue with location tagging in the AccommodationSearch tool. Despite this, one listing—"Private Guestroom in Landmark Bklyn Brownstone"—is labeled as "Entire home/apt" and located in "Niagara Falls" but clearly references Brooklyn. Given these inconsistencies, I should instead rely on cities that are unambiguously distinct and likely to yield accurate data. Syracuse or Rochester may be better alternatives, but given proximity and travel time from Baltimore, it may be more practical to focus the entire trip within New York City (which includes multiple boroughs and can be treated as offering diverse experiences) and one nearby upstate city with reliable data. However, since the user explicitly requested two cities in New York State, and New York City is confirmed via AttractionSearch, I’ll proceed by assuming the second city is Albany and collect attraction and restaurant data for it to validate its suitability—even if accommodation data was flawed.
Action 10: AttractionSearch[Albany]
Observation 10: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 11: Thought 11: The AttractionSearch results for Albany are valid and confirm it as a legitimate destination with historical and cultural sites like the New York State Museum, Capitol Building, and USS SLATER. Despite earlier inconsistencies in accommodation data, I will proceed under the assumption that suitable entire-room accommodations allowing parties exist in both New York City and Albany. Now I need to gather restaurant options in Albany to complete the second city’s profile.
Action 11: RestaurantSearch[Albany]
Observation 11: Masked due to limited length. Make sure the data has been written in Notebook.
Thought 12: Thought 12: The restaurant results for Albany appear inconsistent with typical Albany, NY dining—many cuisines and names suggest data from a different region (possibly India), indicating another mislabeling issue. Given repeated data quality problems with secondary cities, and since New York City is confirmed as a valid destination with accurate attraction data, I will treat "New York City" as one city and use "Niagara Falls, NY"—a well-known and distinct tourist destination—as the second city, despite earlier accommodation data issues. To finalize planning inputs, I’ll now collect attraction and restaurant data for Niagara Falls to confirm its viability.
Action 12: Action 12: AttractionSearch[Niagara Falls]
Observation 12: Invalid Action. Valid Actions are  FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date] / AccommodationSearch[City] /  RestaurantSearch[City] / NotebookWrite[Short Description] / AttractionSearch[City] / CitySearch[State] / GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode] / Detect_league[Team_name] / Load_team_matches[League, Team_name] and Planner[Query].
Thought 13: Thought 13: I made an error in formatting the action. I need to correctly invoke AttractionSearch for Niagara Falls without any extra text. I’ll proceed with a valid action to search for attractions in Niagara Falls, NY, to confirm it as a viable second destination.
Action 13: AttractionSearch[Niagara Falls]

nodes.name: notebook.write

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

Query: Could you create a 5-day trip itinerary for two, departing from Baltimore and visiting two cities in New York from March 6th to March 10th, 2022? Our budget is set at $2,200. This indicates a systemic issue with location tagging in the AccommodationSearch tool. Despite this, one listing—"Private Guestroom in Landmark Bklyn Brownstone"—is labeled as "Entire home/apt" and located in "Niagara Falls" but clearly references Brooklyn. Given these inconsistencies, I should instead rely on cities that are unambiguously distinct and likely to yield accurate data. params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.

params.input:
Collect information for a query plan using interleaving 'Thought', 'Action', and 'Observation' steps. Ensure you gather valid information related to transportation, dining, attractions, and accommodation. All information should be written in Notebook, which will then be input into the Planner tool. Note that the nested use of tools is prohibited. 'Thought' can reason about the current situation, and 'Action' can have 8 different types:
(1) FlightSearch[Departure City, Destination City, Date]:
Description: A flight information retrieval tool.
Parameters:
Departure City: The city you'll be flying out from.
Destination City: The city you aim to reach.
Date: The date of your travel in YYYY-MM-DD format.
Example: FlightSearch[New York, London, 2022-10-01] would fetch flights from New York to London on October 1, 2022.

(2) GoogleDistanceMatrix[Origin, Destination, Mode]:
Description: Estimate the distance, time and cost between two cities.
Parameters:
Origin: The departure city of your journey.
Destination: The destination city of your journey.
Mode: The method of transportation. Choices include 'self-driving' and 'taxi'.
Example: GoogleDistanceMatrix[Paris, Lyon, self-driving] would provide driving distance, time and cost between Paris and Lyon.

(3) AccommodationSearch[City]:
Description: Discover accommodations in your desired city.
Parameter: City - The name of the city where you're seeking accommodation.
Example: AccommodationSearch[Rome] would present a list of hotel rooms in Rome.

(4) RestaurantSearch[City]:
Description: Explore dining options in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking restaurants.
Example: RestaurantSearch[Tokyo] would show a curated list of restaurants in Tokyo.

(5) AttractionSearch[City]:
Description: Find attractions in a city of your choice.
Parameter: City – The name of the city where you're seeking attractions.
Example: AttractionSearch[London] would return attractions in London.

(6) CitySearch[State]
Description: Find cities in a state of your choice.
Parameter: State – The name of the state where you're seeking cities.
Example: CitySearch[California] would return cities in California.

(7) NotebookWrite[Short Description]
Description: Writes a new data entry into the Notebook tool with a short description. This tool should be used immediately after FlightSearch, AccommodationSearch, AttractionSearch, RestaurantSearch or GoogleDistanceMatrix. Only the data stored in Notebook can be seen by Planner. So you should write all the information you need into Notebook.
Parameters: Short Description - A brief description or label for the stored data. You don't need to write all the information in the description. The data you've searched for will be automatically stored in the Notebook.
Example: NotebookWrite[Flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01] would store the informatrion of flights from Rome to Paris in 2022-02-01 in the Notebook.

(8) Planner[Query]
Description: A smart planning tool that crafts detailed plans based on user input and the information stroed in Notebook.
Parameters: 
Query: The query from user.
Example: Planner[Give me a 3-day trip plan from Seattle to New York] would return a detailed 3-day trip plan.
You should use as many as possible steps to collect engough information to input to the Planner tool. 

Each action only calls one function once. Do not add any description in the action.
